.codex\I-eval-005\gemini_q2_source_content_pool.json:231:    "content": "Fortress North America is replacing free trade in Canada’s lexicon Skip to content Policy Options Search Search fr Economy Fiscal Policy GDP Industrial Policy Inequality Inflation Labour Manufacturing Productivity Transportation Environment Climate Change Energy Sustainable Development Water Management Governing Constitution Democracy Global Affairs Law and Justice National Security Politics Public Service Health Addiction COVID Long-term Care Medical Care Medicare Mental Health Public Health Indigenous Indian Act Indigenous economy Indigenous governance Missing and murdered Reconciliation Science and Technology Artificial intelligence Big Tech Data Innovation R&amp;D Social Policy Child Care Disability Discrimination Education Gender Equality Housing Immigration Language Poverty Welfare More The Functionary Series Podcast Events Letters Economy Fiscal Policy GDP Industrial Policy Inequality Inflation Labour Manufacturing Productivity Transportation Environment Climate Change Energy Sustainable Development Water Management Governing Constitution Democracy Global Affairs Law and Justice National Security Politics Public Service Health Addiction COVID Long-term Care Medical Care Medicare Mental Health Public Health Indigenous Indian Act Indigenous economy Indigenous governance Missing and murdered Reconciliation Science and Technology Artificial intelligence Big Tech Data Innovation R&amp;D Social Policy Child Care Disability Discrimination Education Gender Equality Housing Immigration Language Poverty Welfare More The Functionary Series Podcast Events Letters Search Search fr Commentary Fortress North America is replacing free trade in Canada’s lexicon Canada may save USMCA by adopting Trump’s language, but risk weakening the case for openness everywhere else. May 11, 2026 by Joel Baum Image caption: The cargo vessel Zim Mount Vinson navigates the Savannah River moving toward the Port of Savannah, Thursday, Nov. 13, 2025, in Garden City, near Savannah, Ga. (AP Photo/Mike Stewart) Share Linkedin Icon-bluesky-icon Envelope Print X-twitter Some 143 witnesses sat before officials at public hearings held last year by the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative (USTR) on the future of North American trade. All made their case for the agreement that governs over a trillion dollars in cross-border commerce. They talked about jobs. They talked about factories. They talked about China. They talked about winning.&nbsp; Nobody talked about free trade.&nbsp; My colleagues and I analyzed the full transcript record as well as 993 written submissions filed by organizations in advance. What we found was an absence of terminology so complete that the omission cannot be characterized as a stylistic choice but rather a policy fact. The word &#8220;globalism&#8221; does not appear. &#8220;Comparative advantage&#8221; appears twice. The vocabulary that built the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), which Canada has staked its prosperity on for 30 years, has been scrubbed from the most important trade talks on the continent. Fortress North America replaces free trade One moment from the hearings stays with me. Goldy Hyder, president of the Business Council of Canada, leaned into his microphone and told the room that &#8220;Fortress North America&#8221; was already the phrase American policymakers were using. Ontario Premier Doug Ford had for months been road-testing &#8220;Fortress Am-Can&#8221; to describe a potential “renewed strategic alliance.” By December, U.S. witnesses at the hearing were turning that proposal into a demand. &#8220;We should be building Fortress North America,&#8221; said the American Kitchen Cabinet Alliance. Think about what that phrase does. It takes a decades-long trade agreement, rooted in the idea that lowering barriers between three countries would make all of them richer, and recasts it as a wall. Integration becomes perimeter defence. Speakers offered regional strength against China as motivation where openness once carried the argument. They touted jobs, communities, family budgets and food security where mutual gains from trade once grounded the case. The principled case for open markets was replaced by an appeal to fairness, that is, changing the rules now would be unfair to firms that have already invested to comply. Each substitution sidesteps wording the U.S. administration treats as suspect and replaces it with language President Donald Trump’s administration has declared its own. Trump’s tariffs changed the incentives These substitutions were not freely chosen. Trump has imposed tariffs on Canadian steel, aluminum and softwood lumber and labelled Canada a national security &nbsp;threat . He has also demonstrated through public retaliation that a company defending trade on its own terms comes at a price. For example, the CEO of Walmart was targeted on social media after warning that tariffs would raise prices. &#8220;Fortress North America&#8221; is not a creative rebranding exercise. It is what remains available after the old vocabulary has been made dangerous to use for any company or industry group with business before the administration. The result is a public record that reads as unanimous. The National Association of Manufacturers said, &#8220;When manufacturing wins, America wins.&#8221; Even American labour unions who want the agreement renegotiated accepted the new terms. They argued that the United-States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA), negotiated during Trump’s first term had failed workers and communities. How Canada can prepare for high-pressure U.S. demands on trade Rethinking retaliation in the age of Trump But they made that argument on the administration’s terms: the agreement should be judged by what it delivers for workers, not by whether it advances open trade. Everyone involved in USTR proceedings – even the opposition – is speaking the same trade language now, because a political order with its own logic and its own enforcement has replaced the old language. Canada’s new language comes with long-term costs Here is what keeps me up at night about this. The vocabulary Canada endorsed in Washington does not stay in Washington. &#8220;Fortress North America&#8221; is a framework that defines Canada&#8217;s value to the United States as conditional on continuous alignment with American economic security priorities. Ford&#8217;s Am-Can growth plan says it explicitly: Any USMCA member should &#8220;match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles and other strategically important products or lose its seat at the table.&#8221; That sentence was written by Canadians. It will be quoted back to them. Prime Minister Mark Carney has staked Canada&#8217;s trade future on diversification — new agreements with Europe, the United Kingdom and partners across Asia and the Pacific. Every one of those negotiations will require Canadian officials to argue that openness and rules-based arrangements serve all participants. There are no alternative terms for what Carney is trying to do. Yet those are the terms that Canada&#8217;s most prominent business voices in Washington have spent the past year helping to discredit. The fortress argument is already turning against Canada Washington is already using the new language against Canada. In April, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer told Congress that Canada is &#8220;doubling down on globalization,&#8221; while the United States is trying to correct for the failures the administration feels globalization has caused. He also said the two approaches &#8220;don&#8217;t fit together very well.&#8221; He was talking about Carney&#8217;s pursuit of new trade agreements in Europe and Asia. The language Canada has helped install in Washington is now being turned against Canada’s own trade strategy. Greer used it to cast diversification as incompatible with the North American relationship Canada is trying to preserve. Canada did not choose this contradiction. The coercion was real, the threats were specific and the fortress vocabulary may yet save the agreement when it is reviewed. But terminology endorsed by Canadian officials under duress does not expire if that pressure lifts. Every month that vocabulary succeeds in Washington, it becomes harder to rebuild a case for everything Canada needs beyond North America. The risk is that Canada saves one agreement by discrediting the arguments it needs for all the others. Someone in Ottawa should be calculating that price. That means tracking, agreement by agreement, how the language Canada has endorsed in Washington constrains arguments Canadian negotiators can make in Brussels, London and Tokyo. The Trump administration is not going to calculate the price for us. Do you have something to say about the article you just read? Be part of the Policy Options discussion, and send in your own submission. Here is a link on how to do it. Joel Baum Joel Baum is a professor at the University of Toronto’s Rotman School of Management who studies competition, co-operation, knowledge, trust, and narrative in emerging markets, public discourse, and digital communication, including media and algorithmic systems. Fortress North America is replacing free trade in Canada’s lexicon by&nbsp;Joel Baum&nbsp; Originally published on Policy Options May 11, 2026 &lt;h1&gt;Fortress North America is replacing free trade in Canada’s lexicon&lt;/h1&gt; &lt;p class=&quot;byline&quot;&gt;by&nbsp;Joel Baum&nbsp; &lt;br /&gt;Originally published on &lt;em&gt;Policy Options&lt;/em&gt; &lt;br /&gt;May 11, 2026&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Some 143 witnesses sat before officials at public hearings held last year by the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative (USTR) on the future of North American trade. All made their case for the agreement that governs over a trillion dollars in cross-border commerce. They talked about jobs. They talked about factories. They talked about China. They talked about winning.&amp;nbsp;&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Nobody talked about free trade.&amp;nbsp;&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;My colleagues and I analyzed the full transcript&lt;a href=\"https://ustr.gov/about/policy-offices/press-office/press-releases/2025/december/public-hearing-first-joint-review-usmca\"&gt; record&lt;/a&gt; as well as 993 written&lt;a href=\"https://comments.ustr.gov/s/docket?docketNumber=USTR-2022-0001\"&gt; submissions&lt;/a&gt; filed by organizations in advance. What we found was an absence of terminology so complete that the omission cannot be characterized as a stylistic choice but rather a policy fact. The word \"globalism\" does not appear. \"Comparative advantage\" appears twice. The vocabulary that built the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), which Canada has staked its prosperity on for 30 years, has been scrubbed from the most important trade talks on the continent.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;h2 class=\"wp-block-heading\"&gt;Fortress North America replaces free trade&lt;/h2&gt; &lt;p&gt;One moment from the hearings stays with me. Goldy Hyder, president of the Business Council of Canada, leaned into his microphone and told the room that \"Fortress North America\" was already the phrase American policymakers were using. Ontario Premier Doug Ford had for months been&lt;a href=\"https://news.ontario.ca/en/release/1005542/ontario-building-fortress-am-can-to-power-economic-prosperity-in-canada-and-the-united-states\"&gt; road-testing&lt;/a&gt; \"Fortress Am-Can\" to describe a potential “renewed strategic alliance.” By December, U.S. witnesses at the hearing were turning that proposal into a demand. \"We should be building Fortress North America,\" said the American Kitchen Cabinet Alliance.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Think about what that phrase does. It takes a decades-long trade agreement, rooted in the idea that lowering barriers between three countries would make all of them richer, and recasts it as a wall. Integration becomes perimeter defence. Speakers offered regional strength against China as motivation where openness once carried the argument. They touted jobs, communities, family budgets and food security where mutual gains from trade once grounded the case.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;The principled case for open markets was replaced by an appeal to fairness, that is, changing the rules now would be unfair to firms that have already invested to comply. Each substitution sidesteps wording the U.S. administration treats as suspect and replaces it with language President Donald Trump’s administration has declared its own.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;h2 class=\"wp-block-heading\"&gt;Trump’s tariffs changed the incentives&lt;/h2&gt; &lt;p&gt;These substitutions were not freely chosen. Trump has imposed tariffs on Canadian steel, aluminum and softwood lumber and labelled Canada a national security &lt;a href=\"https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/british-columbia/donald-trump-section-232-investigation-softwood-lumber-1.7616425\"&gt;&amp;nbsp;threat&lt;/a&gt;. He has also demonstrated through public retaliation that a company defending trade on its own terms comes at a price. For example, the CEO of Walmart was targeted on &lt;a href=\"https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/114523638623110397)__\"&gt;social media&lt;/a&gt; after warning that tariffs would raise prices. \"Fortress North America\" is not a creative rebranding exercise. It is what remains available after the old vocabulary has been made dangerous to use for any company or industry group with business before the administration.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;The result is a public record that reads as unanimous. The National Association of Manufacturers said, \"When manufacturing wins, America wins.\" Even American labour unions who want the agreement renegotiated accepted the new terms. They argued that the United-States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA), negotiated during Trump’s first term had failed workers and communities.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;blockquote class=\"wp-block-quote\"&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;a href=\"https://policyoptions.irpp.org/2026/03/us-canada-decisionmaking/\"&gt;How Canada can prepare for high-pressure U.S. demands on trade&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;a href=\"https://policyoptions.irpp.org/2026/04/canada-us-retaliation-trump-guideposts/\" class=\"\"&gt;Rethinking retaliation in the age of Trump&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/p&gt; &lt;/blockquote&gt; &lt;p&gt;But they made that argument on the administration’s terms: the agreement should be judged by what it delivers for workers, not by whether it advances open trade. Everyone involved in USTR proceedings – even the opposition – is speaking the same trade language now, because a political order with its own logic and its own enforcement has replaced the old language.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;h2 class=\"wp-block-heading\"&gt;Canada’s new language comes with long-term costs&lt;/h2&gt; &lt;p&gt;Here is what keeps me up at night about this. The vocabulary Canada endorsed in Washington does not stay in Washington. \"Fortress North America\" is a framework that defines Canada's value to the United States as conditional on continuous alignment with American economic security priorities. Ford's &lt;a href=\"https://www.ontario.ca/page/building-fortress-am-can-ontarios-am-can-growth-plan\"&gt;Am-Can&lt;/a&gt; growth plan says it explicitly: Any USMCA member should \"match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles and other strategically important products or lose its seat at the table.\" That sentence was written by Canadians. It will be quoted back to them.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Prime Minister Mark Carney has staked Canada's trade future on diversification — new agreements with Europe, the United Kingdom and partners across Asia and the Pacific. Every one of those negotiations will require Canadian officials to argue that openness and rules-based arrangements serve all participants. There are no alternative terms for what Carney is trying to do. In April, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer told &lt;a href=\"https://globalnews.ca/news/11812378/canada-doubling-down-globalization-at-odds-u-s-trade-goal-greer/\"&gt;Congress&lt;/a&gt; that Canada is \"doubling down on globalization,\" while the United States is trying to correct for the failures the administration feels globalization has caused. He also said the two approaches \"don't fit together very well.\" He was talking about Carney's pursuit of new trade agreements in Europe and Asia. Greer used it to cast diversification as incompatible with the North American relationship Canada is trying to preserve.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Canada did not choose this contradiction. Every month that vocabulary succeeds in Washington, it becomes harder to rebuild a case for everything Canada needs beyond North America.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;The risk is that Canada saves one agreement by discrediting the arguments it needs for all the others. Someone in Ottawa should be calculating that price. That means tracking, agreement by agreement, how the language Canada has endorsed in Washington constrains arguments Canadian negotiators can make in Brussels, London and Tokyo. The Trump administration is not going to calculate the price for us.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;This &amp;lt;a target=&amp;quot;_blank&amp;quot; href=&amp;quot;https://policyoptions.irpp.org/2026/05/fortress-trade-language/&amp;quot;&amp;gt;article&amp;lt;/a&amp;gt; first appeared on &amp;lt;a target=&amp;quot;_blank&amp;quot; href=&amp;quot;https://policyoptions.irpp.org&amp;quot;&amp;gt;Policy Options&amp;lt;/a&amp;gt; and is republished here under a Creative Commons license.&amp;lt;img src=&amp;quot;https://policyoptions.irpp.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/po_favicon-150x150.png&amp;quot; style=&amp;quot;width:1em;height:1em;margin-left:10px;&amp;quot;&amp;gt;&amp;lt;img id=&amp;quot;republication-tracker-tool-source&amp;quot; src=&amp;quot;https://policyoptions.irpp.org/?republication-pixel=true&amp;amp;post=304543&amp;quot; style=&amp;quot;width:1px;height:1px;&amp;quot;&amp;gt;&lt;/p&gt; Copy to Clipboard This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License . 1 Joel Baum Joel Baum is a professor at the University of Toronto’s Rotman School of Management who studies competition, co-operation, knowledge, trust, and narrative in emerging markets, public discourse, and digital communication, including media and algorithmic systems. Hide message bar 5:47 Carney promotes ‘Build Canada’ agenda at Global Progress summit WATCH: Prime Minister Mark Carney laid out a broad vision for Canada&rsquo;s future at the Global Progress Action Summit in Toronto, focusing on housing, trade, artificial intelligence (AI) and economic sovereignty. Carney said Canada must &ldquo;build again&rdquo; by investing in affordable housing, reducing reliance on the United States, and expanding strategic industries amid ongoing tariff pressures. He also defended government spending cuts and promised a forthcoming national AI strategy focused on &ldquo;safe and sovereign&rdquo; technology that would benefit Canadians. Leave a comment Share this item on Facebook Share this item via WhatsApp Share this item on X Send this page to someone via email See more sharing options Share Close Share this item on Facebook facebook Share this item on X x Send this page to someone via email email Share this item via WhatsApp whatsapp Share this item via Telegram telegram Share this item on Reddit reddit Share this item on BlueSky bluesky Copy article link Copy link Descrease article font size Increase article font size Political leaders and policy experts from around the world gathered in Toronto on Saturday for the 2026 Global Progress Action Summit. The summit, co-hosted by Canada 2020 and the Center for American Progress Action Fund (CAPAF), is a day-long event focused on economic security, democracy and global co-operation, according to a release by the CAPAF. It featured Prime Minister Mark Carney alongside several federal cabinet ministers, including Industry Minister Mélanie Joly, Foreign Affairs Minister Anita Anand and Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne. Former U.S. president Barack Obama was also in attendance. Joly started off the summit Saturday morning, hosting a discussion with former U.S. transportation secretary Pete Buttigieg on rebuilding trust through economic security. During the discussion, Joly spoke about the global impact of U.S. policies. Story continues below advertisement “What is going on in the U.S. doesn’t stay in the U.S.,” she said. “It has an impact across the world.” Get breaking National news Get breaking Canada news delivered to your inbox as it happens so you won&#039;t miss a trending story. Sign up for breaking National newsletter Get Started By providing your email address, you have read and agree to Global News' Terms and Conditions and Privacy Policy . “If the U.S. democracy is not working well, it has an impact on all democracies,” she added. Foreign Affairs Minister Anita Anand developed the idea during a panel on the ever-changing Canada-U.S. relations. “Canadians and Americans on both sides of the border want to see our countries grow together for decades and decades to come,” Anand said. &#8220;Trade diversification is a means of de-risking in this moment where there is a complete breakdown of the global trading order,&#8221; she said. Speaking later in the day, Carney addressed Canada’s economic relationship with the United States amid ongoing tariffs on sectors including steel, aluminum and autos. “We still have the best trade deal with the United States,” Carney said. “Over 85 per cent of our goods move tariff-free across the border.” More on Canada More videos Saskatchewan officials urge caution as dry weather triggers wildfires Prairies Premier League soccer kicks off in Saskatoon Majority of Canadians say energy policy should focus on economic growth: poll B.C. police women want lawsuit, not labour arbitration, over alleged discrimination Canadians stranded on hantavirus cruise ship return home to isolate Stay away from rodents: Canada’s top doctor offers guidance on hantavirus precautions West Island volunteer service New otters arrive at the Edmonton Valley Zoo He said Canada remains open to “deeper integration” with the U.S., including what he described as a possible “Fortress North America” approach in select sectors. However, Carney said Canada is also prepared to expand elsewhere if deeper integration is not possible. Story continues below advertisement “If that route is not ultimately possible, we will invest heavily in new markets and products,” he said. “We will reward those who build, buy and produce in Canada.” Carney also spoke about artificial intelligence and Canada’s upcoming AI strategy, saying Canadians want technology that is “safe and sovereign” and creates economic opportunities while strengthening public services. In closing remarks, Carney called for bold economic action amid global uncertainty. “We have to take risks again,” he said. “Because in a crisis, fortune favors the bold.” U.S. President Donald Trump did not attend the summit. Other sessions throughout the day focused on foreign affairs, artificial intelligence, digital transformation and democratic institutions, according to the organizers. Meanwhile, some supporters are saying the summit highlights international collaboration on shared economic and political challenges. .codex\I-eval-005\gemini_q2_source_content_pool.json:607:    "content": "Critical minerals strategy risks shifting reliance from China to U.S. Skip to content Policy Options Search Search fr Economy Fiscal Policy GDP Industrial Policy Inequality Inflation Labour Manufacturing Productivity Transportation Environment Climate Change Energy Sustainable Development Water Management Governing Constitution Democracy Global Affairs Law and Justice National Security Politics Public Service Health Addiction COVID Long-term Care Medical Care Medicare Mental Health Public Health Indigenous Indian Act Indigenous economy Indigenous governance Missing and murdered Reconciliation Science and Technology Artificial intelligence Big Tech Data Innovation R&amp;D Social Policy Child Care Disability Discrimination Education Gender Equality Housing Immigration Language Poverty Welfare More The Functionary Series Podcast Events Letters Economy Fiscal Policy GDP Industrial Policy Inequality Inflation Labour Manufacturing Productivity Transportation Environment Climate Change Energy Sustainable Development Water Management Governing Constitution Democracy Global Affairs Law and Justice National Security Politics Public Service Health Addiction COVID Long-term Care Medical Care Medicare Mental Health Public Health Indigenous Indian Act Indigenous economy Indigenous governance Missing and murdered Reconciliation Science and Technology Artificial intelligence Big Tech Data Innovation R&amp;D Social Policy Child Care Disability Discrimination Education Gender Equality Housing Immigration Language Poverty Welfare More The Functionary Series Podcast Events Letters Search Search fr Commentary Can a U.S.-led critical minerals strategy deliver for climate and Canada? U.S. efforts to build allied supply chains could weaken China’s dominance but risk tying partners like Canada more tightly to American priorities, which do not include climate action. March 4, 2026 by Philippe Le Billon Raphaël Deberdt Image caption: Photographers capture the group of ministers as they gather for a photo during the Critical Minerals Ministerial meeting at the State Department, Wednesday, Feb. 4, 2026 in Washington. Canada’s Foreign Affairs Minister Anita Anand is in the middle of the back grow in a burgundy suit. (AP Photo/Kevin Wolf) Share Linkedin Icon-bluesky-icon Envelope Print X-twitter In February, the United States held critical minerals talks with 54 countries, including Canada, as well as the European Union. The stated goal was to counter China’s current grip on these minerals so crucial to green technologies by increasing international collaboration and stockpiling a separate supply. &nbsp;&nbsp; Vice-president JD Vance called for a team approach and a “united front” against China. It’s a drastic departure from President Donald Trump’s unilateral policies that have been punishing long-term allies or threatening to take over their territory, as seen with Canada and Greenland . Though welcome in principle, this new “critical minerals security” risks simply shifting reliance onto the U.S. from China. Nor does it necessarily lead to an effective transition to clean energy since Trump still maintains a pro-fossil-fuels stand . China’s strategic chokepoint Many producers around the world can supply critical minerals. Canada is one of them. But China dominates roughly 90 per cent of global rare earth processing capacity as it does graphite, lithium, cobalt and other materials. This makes China not just a supplier, but also the “factory floor” for key high-tech products — including batteries, magnets and electronics — that depend on these minerals. China has also consolidated its position with export controls and strenuous licensing requirements on Chinese-sourced minerals and processing technologies. This has complicated western attempts to diversify supply chains or bring production to other countries like Canada. Fragmented responses and alternative markets Over the past fifteen years, western governments have experimented with a range of responses to dependence on China. There have been subsidies for domestic extraction and processing, bilateral deals to underwrite projects abroad and multilateral co-operation such as the Minerals Security Partnership . The former Biden administration was keen on extending U.S. leadership through several executive orders and partnerships. Agreements and memorandums of understanding were signed with Canada, South Korea and India, among others. Yet these efforts only partially dented the underlying imbalance in the critical minerals market. Production costs, market prices and access to processing technologies have been perennial challenges. The U.S., EU and many non-Chinese producers face higher environmental, regulatory and labour costs and must also contend with China dumping heavily discounted products into their markets, thereby undercutting new production. Subsidies have been poorly integrated and are often blamed for “start-and-stop” investment cycles in mining and refining. Moreover, western electric vehicle markets are volatile as consumer demand ebbs and flows in response to incentives and broader economic cycles. This has distorted market signals — sometimes undermining investment confidence in new supply chains. Local resistance to mining projects has further constrained production, especially in Europe where community acceptance remains fragile. From fragmentation to a U.S.-led strategy Agreements last October between the U.S. and Australia and the U.S. and Japan , as well as multilateral discussions emerging from critical minerals talks, parallel the previous administration’s strategy. But they also signal a shift towards more structured co-operation. These agreements envision co-ordinated investment, streamlined permitting, shared project selection and joint financing to create alternative supply corridors. In theory, this unified approach could speed up diversification and reduce supply disruption, but it also has significant risks: Greater dependence on the U.S: Co-operation between countries through U.S.-led initiatives like Project VAULT (a strategic minerals reserve for companies) and FORGE (a multilateral investment mechanism) could simply shift reliance from one superpower to another. The same could be said of bilateral frameworks that emphasize U.S. financial support and policies. Smaller partners and middle powers like Canada, finding themselves tied more closely to American policy priorities, could face reduced leverage. Selective privileging: U.S. willingness to take equity stakes in strategic minerals companies — as signalled by a bill proposing a US$2.5 billion stockpile of critical minerals — favours U.S.-based enterprises. This can skew competition and favour U.S. companies over Canadian ones, for example. Negative market perceptions: Early evidence suggests that shifts in policy signals can depress the market valuation of firms previously buoyed by geopolitical supply concerns. Some Western mining companies experienced declines in share prices as U.S. policy actions reduced perceived supply risk premiums. This paradox of policy success undermining investment incentives is an underexplored risk. Marginalized European autonomy: Despite participation in discussions, the European Union remains less central to the U.S. bid to secure critical minerals and technology. Recent initiatives signal ambitions for continental strategic autonomy. The EU seems to maintain interest for now in partnering with the U.S. but at the risk of marginalizing the EU’s own supply chain structures. Reduced trade leverage with the U.S.: Hiving off critical minerals in a separate accord could eliminate a major point of influence for some countries, especially Canada and Mexico , in broader trade agreements. Market destabilization: Building a secure store of critical minerals could hurt minerals markets. While these efforts seek to provide user industries with a buffer against disruptions at mine sites, stockpiling is often perceived as a form of market manipulation when governments do not disclose or alter reported supply volumes. This creates uncertainty and market instability. A strategic mismatch Underlying these geopolitical strategies is a deeper contradiction: Washington wants to secure critical minerals without reducing fossil fuel production. This will not address the climate crisis, which Trump has repeatedly presented as a hoax. &nbsp; So what would be a more coherent and equitable approach that could benefit Canada? Redefining sovereignty in the&nbsp;critical&nbsp;minerals&nbsp;era New approach needed in mining&nbsp;critical&nbsp;minerals First, policymakers must recognize supply chains as more than geopolitical bargaining chips. Secure access should be paired with investment in recycling, substitution ad demand-side efficiency. These are areas where China’s dominance should be challenged and the competitiveness of western firms increased. Second, challenging China’s dominance also requires thinking about the future in a technology-driven world. With current and fast shifts in mineral uses, western countries, including Canada, must assess new needs instead of focusing on a catch-up strategy. Third, collaboration on critical minerals must evolve beyond bilateral frameworks so as to include non-U.S. countries in governance, financing and technical standards. This would help prevent new levels of dependency. Fourth, climate and industrial policy goals must be reconciled. Supporting fossil fuel extraction while stockpiling minerals for clean energy is contradictory . It neither encourages a move away from oil, coal and natural gas nor builds durable supply chains. Finally, the role of critical minerals must be clarified. Are these minerals to be used in the transition to green technologies or for military buildups? How do we prioritize them, especially when military and civilian uses overlap? And how can security of access be ensured given unprecedented tensions within NATO? Ultimately, the geopolitics of critical minerals reveals a paradox that Canada must acknowledge. The very effort by countries to secure supply and reduce their vulnerability can itself entrench new forms of strategic reliance on the United States. Breaking China’s dominance requires more than subsidies , frameworks and summits. It demands an integrated industrial and climate strategy in which markets, countries and alliances share long-term objectives. Do you have something to say about the article you just read? Be part of the Policy Options discussion, and send in your own submission. Here is a link on how to do it. Philippe Le Billon Philippe Le Billon is a professor at the school of public policy and global affair at the University of British Columbia. He is the co-author of Oil (Polity Press) and Wars of Plunder (OUP). More from this author Raphaël Deberdt Raphaël Deberdt is a post-doctoral student at the Copenhagen Business School. Can a U.S.-led critical minerals strategy deliver for climate and Canada? by&nbsp;Philippe Le Billon, Raphaël Deberdt&nbsp; Originally published on Policy Options March 4, 2026 &lt;h1&gt;Can a U.S.-led critical minerals strategy deliver for climate and Canada?&lt;/h1&gt; &lt;p class=&quot;byline&quot;&gt;by&nbsp;Philippe Le Billon, Raphaël Deberdt&nbsp; &lt;br /&gt;Originally published on &lt;em&gt;Policy Options&lt;/em&gt; &lt;br /&gt;March 4, 2026&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;In February, the United States held critical minerals &lt;a href=\"https://www.state.gov/releases/2026/02/2026-critical-minerals-ministerial/\"&gt;talks&lt;/a&gt; with 54 countries, including Canada, as well as the European Union. The stated goal was to counter China’s current grip on these minerals so &lt;a href=\"https://www.iea.org/reports/the-role-of-critical-minerals-in-clean-energy-transitions/executive-summary\"&gt;crucial&lt;/a&gt; to green technologies by increasing international collaboration and stockpiling a separate supply. &amp;nbsp;&amp;nbsp;&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Vice-president JD Vance called for a team approach and a “united front” against China. It’s a drastic &lt;a href=\"https://www.atlanticcouncil.org/dispatches/us-critical-minerals-policy-goes-collaborative-with-forge/\"&gt;departure&lt;/a&gt; from President Donald Trump’s unilateral policies that have been punishing long-term allies or threatening to take over their territory, as seen with &lt;a href=\"https://www.policymagazine.ca/trump-is-a-threat-to-canada-but-china-is-not-the-answer/\"&gt;Canada&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href=\"https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c74x4m71pmjo\"&gt;Greenland&lt;/a&gt;.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Though welcome in principle, this new “critical minerals security” risks simply shifting reliance onto the U.S. from China. Nor does it necessarily lead to an effective transition to clean energy since Trump still maintains a pro-fossil-fuels&lt;a href=\"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214629625002506\"&gt; stand&lt;/a&gt;.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;h2 class=\"wp-block-heading\"&gt;China’s strategic chokepoint&lt;/h2&gt; &lt;p&gt;Many producers around the world can supply critical minerals.&lt;a href=\"https://www.iea.org/commentaries/canada-is-set-to-play-a-leading-role-in-supplying-the-world-with-responsibly-produced-critical-minerals\"&gt; Canada&lt;/a&gt; is one of them. But &lt;a href=\"https://www.iea.org/reports/global-critical-minerals-outlook-2025\"&gt;China&lt;/a&gt; dominates roughly 90 per cent of global rare earth processing capacity as it does graphite, lithium, cobalt and other materials. This makes China not just a supplier, but also the “factory floor” for key high-tech products — including batteries, magnets and electronics — that depend on these minerals.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;China has also consolidated its position with &lt;a href=\"https://www.iea.org/commentaries/with-new-export-controls-on-critical-minerals-supply-concentration-risks-become-reality\"&gt;export controls&lt;/a&gt; and strenuous licensing requirements on Chinese-sourced minerals and processing &lt;a href=\"https://www.lemonde.fr/en/economy/article/2025/10/09/rare-earths-china-curbs-exports-of-key-processing-technologies_6746245_19.html\"&gt;technologies.&lt;/a&gt; This has complicated western attempts to diversify supply chains or bring production to other countries like Canada.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;h2 class=\"wp-block-heading\"&gt;Fragmented responses and alternative markets&lt;/h2&gt; &lt;p&gt;Over the past fifteen years, western governments have experimented with a range of&lt;a href=\"https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/data-tools/critical-minerals-policy-tracker\"&gt; responses&lt;/a&gt; to dependence on China. There have been subsidies for domestic extraction and processing, bilateral deals to underwrite projects abroad and multilateral co-operation such as the &lt;a href=\"https://ca.usembassy.gov/minerals-security-partnership/\"&gt;Minerals Security Partnership&lt;/a&gt;. The former Biden administration was keen on &lt;a href=\"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214629625002506#:~:text=The%20Biden%20presidency,through%20production%20subsidies\"&gt;extending&lt;/a&gt; U.S. leadership through several executive orders and partnerships. &lt;a href=\"https://www.csis.org/analysis/prospecting-partners-case-bilateral-cooperation-critical-minerals\"&gt;Agreements&lt;/a&gt; and memorandums of understanding were signed with Canada, South Korea and India, among others. Yet these efforts only partially dented the underlying imbalance in the critical minerals market.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Production costs, market prices and access to processing technologies have been perennial challenges. The U.S., EU and many non-Chinese producers face higher environmental, regulatory and labour &lt;a href=\"https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13563-022-00340-4\"&gt;costs&lt;/a&gt; and must also contend with China dumping heavily discounted products into their markets, thereby undercutting new production.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Subsidies have been poorly integrated and are often blamed for “start-and-stop” investment cycles in mining and refining. Moreover, western electric vehicle markets are volatile as consumer demand ebbs and flows in response to incentives and broader economic cycles. This has distorted market signals — sometimes undermining investment confidence in new supply chains. Local &lt;a href=\"https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-024-01944-0\"&gt;resistance&lt;/a&gt; to mining projects has further constrained production, especially in&lt;a href=\"https://doi.org/10.1016/j.exis.2025.101625\"&gt; Europe&lt;/a&gt; where community acceptance remains fragile.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;h2 class=\"wp-block-heading\"&gt;From fragmentation to a U.S.-led strategy&lt;/h2&gt; &lt;p&gt;Agreements last October between the U.S. and &lt;a href=\"https://www.industry.gov.au/publications/united-states-australia-framework-securing-supply-mining-and-processing-critical-minerals-and-rare-earths\"&gt;Australia&lt;/a&gt; and the U.S. and &lt;a href=\"https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefings-statements/2025/10/united-states-japan-framework-for-securing-the-supply-of-critical-minerals-and-rare-earths-through-mining-and-processing/\"&gt;Japan&lt;/a&gt;, as well as multilateral discussions emerging from critical minerals talks, parallel the previous administration’s strategy. But they also signal a shift towards more structured co-operation. These agreements envision co-ordinated investment, streamlined permitting, shared project selection and joint financing to create alternative supply corridors.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;In theory, this unified approach could speed up diversification and reduce supply disruption, but it also has significant risks:&lt;/p&gt; &lt;ul class=\"wp-block-list\"&gt; &lt;li&gt;Greater dependence on the U.S: Co-operation between countries through U.S.-led initiatives like Project &lt;a href=\"https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/feb/03/trump-critical-minerals-stockpile-project-vault\"&gt;VAULT&lt;/a&gt; (a strategic minerals reserve for companies) and &lt;a href=\"https://www.koreatimes.co.kr/foreignaffairs/20260205/s-korea-to-chair-forge-critical-minerals-initiative-through-june-us-state-dept\"&gt;FORGE&lt;/a&gt; (a multilateral investment mechanism) could simply shift reliance from one superpower to another. The same could be said of bilateral frameworks that emphasize U.S. financial support and policies. Smaller partners and middle powers like Canada, finding themselves tied more closely to American policy priorities, could face reduced leverage.&lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt; &lt;ul class=\"wp-block-list\"&gt; &lt;li&gt;Selective privileging: U.S. willingness to take equity stakes in strategic minerals companies — as signalled by a &lt;a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/legal/litigation/us-lawmakers-introduce-bill-create-25-billion-critical-minerals-stockpile-2026-01-15/\"&gt;bill&lt;/a&gt; proposing a US$2.5 billion stockpile of critical minerals — favours U.S.-based enterprises. This can skew competition and favour U.S. companies over Canadian ones, for example.&lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt; &lt;ul class=\"wp-block-list\"&gt; &lt;li&gt;Negative market perceptions: Early evidence suggests that shifts in policy signals can depress the market valuation of firms previously buoyed by geopolitical supply concerns. Some Western mining companies experienced &lt;a href=\"https://www.barrons.com/articles/mp-stock-vance-rubio-critical-minerals-cbb64637\"&gt;declines&lt;/a&gt; in share prices as U.S. policy actions reduced perceived supply risk premiums. This paradox of policy success undermining investment incentives is an underexplored risk.&lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt; &lt;ul class=\"wp-block-list\"&gt; &lt;li&gt;Marginalized European autonomy: Despite participation in discussions, the European Union remains less central to the U.S. bid to secure critical minerals and technology. Recent &lt;a href=\"https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/ip_25_2891\"&gt;initiatives&lt;/a&gt; signal ambitions for continental strategic autonomy. The EU seems to maintain interest for now in &lt;a href=\"https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/fr/statement_26_323\"&gt;partnering&lt;/a&gt; with the U.S. but at the risk of marginalizing the EU’s own supply chain structures.&lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt; &lt;ul class=\"wp-block-list\"&gt; &lt;li&gt;Reduced trade leverage with the U.S.: Hiving off critical minerals in a separate accord could eliminate a major point of influence for some countries, especially &lt;a href=\"https://www.theglobeandmail.com/business/article-us-critical-minerals-bloc-usmca-anand/\"&gt;Canada and Mexico&lt;/a&gt;, in broader trade agreements.&lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt; &lt;ul class=\"wp-block-list\"&gt; &lt;li&gt;Market destabilization: Building a secure store of critical minerals could hurt minerals markets. While these efforts seek to provide user industries with a buffer against disruptions at mine sites, stockpiling is often perceived as a form of &lt;a href=\"https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eneco.2025.108496\"&gt;market manipulation&lt;/a&gt; when governments do not disclose or alter reported supply volumes. This creates uncertainty and market instability.&lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt; &lt;h2 class=\"wp-block-heading\"&gt;A strategic mismatch&lt;/h2&gt; &lt;p&gt;Underlying these geopolitical strategies is a deeper contradiction: Washington wants to secure critical minerals without reducing fossil fuel production. This will not address the climate crisis, which Trump has repeatedly presented as a &lt;a href=\"https://www.cfr.org/articles/trumps-un-speech-cannot-steer-global-climate-effort\"&gt;hoax. &lt;/a&gt;&amp;nbsp;&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;So what would be a more coherent and equitable approach that could benefit Canada?&lt;/p&gt; &lt;blockquote class=\"wp-block-quote\"&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;a href=\"https://policyoptions.irpp.org/2025/11/sovereignty-critical-minerals/\"&gt;Redefining sovereignty in the&amp;nbsp;critical&amp;nbsp;minerals&amp;nbsp;era&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/p&gt; &lt;/blockquote&gt; &lt;blockquote class=\"wp-block-quote\"&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;a href=\"https://policyoptions.irpp.org/2025/08/mining-critical-minerals/\"&gt;New approach needed in mining&amp;nbsp;critical&amp;nbsp;minerals&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/p&gt; &lt;/blockquote&gt; &lt;p&gt;First, policymakers must recognize supply chains as more than geopolitical bargaining chips. Secure access should be paired with &lt;a href=\"https://www.fastmarkets.com/insights/china-lithium-ion-battery-recycling/\"&gt;investment&lt;/a&gt; in recycling, substitution ad demand-side efficiency. These are areas where China’s dominance should be challenged and the competitiveness of western firms increased.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Second, challenging China’s dominance also requires thinking about the future in a technology-driven world. With current and fast&lt;a href=\"https://www.benzinga.com/markets/tech/26/02/50472792/tesla-rival-byd-unveils-new-battery-tech-promising-10000-cycle-lifespan-eyes-solid-state-production-by-2027\"&gt; shifts&lt;/a&gt; in mineral uses, western countries, including Canada, must assess new needs instead of focusing on a catch-up strategy.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Third, collaboration on critical minerals must evolve beyond bilateral frameworks so as to include non-U.S. countries in governance, financing and technical standards. This would help prevent new levels of dependency.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Fourth, climate and industrial policy goals must be reconciled. Supporting fossil fuel extraction while stockpiling minerals for clean energy is &lt;a href=\"https://www.hurstpublishers.com/book/the-great-green-grab/\"&gt;contradictory&lt;/a&gt;. It neither encourages a move away from oil, coal and natural gas nor builds durable supply chains.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Finally, the role of critical minerals must be clarified. Are these minerals &lt;a href=\"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2214790X25002254?casa_token=G7K7c2TRRgAAAAAA:37jFIjoKomU9L8MwZrPWAqA2OGxCSLV9ytHZekAuWhTzUpEBO2tH5Vtq1x28y5BzhyOmAP-BrS8\"&gt;to be used&lt;/a&gt; in the transition to green technologies or for military buildups? How do we prioritize them, especially when military and civilian uses overlap? And how can security of access be ensured given unprecedented tensions within NATO?&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;Ultimately, the geopolitics of critical minerals reveals a paradox that Canada must acknowledge. The very effort by countries to secure supply and reduce their vulnerability can itself entrench new forms of strategic reliance on the United States. Breaking China’s dominance requires more than &lt;a href=\"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589811625000527/pdfft?md5=840b5a9db7673e5c4682155d2406e9c0&amp;amp;pid=1-s2.0-S2589811625000527-main.pdf\"&gt;subsidies&lt;/a&gt;, frameworks and summits. It demands an integrated industrial and climate strategy in which markets, &lt;a href=\"https://doi.org/10.1038/s41560-025-01940-4\"&gt;countries&lt;/a&gt; and alliances share long-term objectives.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;This &amp;lt;a target=&amp;quot;_blank&amp;quot; href=&amp;quot;https://policyoptions.irpp.org/2026/03/critical-minerals-us/&amp;quot;&amp;gt;article&amp;lt;/a&amp;gt; first appeared on &amp;lt;a target=&amp;quot;_blank&amp;quot; href=&amp;quot;https://policyoptions.irpp.org&amp;quot;&amp;gt;Policy Options&amp;lt;/a&amp;gt; and is republished here under a Creative Commons license.&amp;lt;img src=&amp;quot;https://policyoptions.irpp.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/po_favicon-150x150.png&amp;quot; style=&amp;quot;width:1em;height:1em;margin-left:10px;&amp;quot;&amp;gt;&amp;lt;img id=&amp;quot;republication-tracker-tool-source&amp;quot; src=&amp;quot;https://policyoptions.irpp.org/?republication-pixel=true&amp;amp;post=303644&amp;quot; style=&amp;quot;width:1px;height:1px;&amp;quot;&amp;gt;&lt;/p&gt; Copy to Clipboard This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License . 1 Philippe Le Billon Philippe Le Billon is a professor at the school of public policy and global affair at the University of British Columbia. outputs\polaris_graph\WEB_20260430T215622_beceeb.json:103:  "contradictions": [],
outputs\polaris_graph\WEB_20260430T215622_beceeb.json:135:  "final_report": "# Research report: What is BPEI\n\n### Mechanism\n\nCationic branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) functions as a polyelectrolyte with a high positive charge density due to its branched polymer architecture containing numerous amine groups.[1][2] In aqueous solutions at a concentration of 0.1 wt%, BPEI exhibits a surface tension of approximately 71-72 mN m\u207b\u00b9, which is similar to that of water and to other polyelectrolytes like poly(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC).[1] This property contributes to its ability to interact with and disperse materials like reduced graphite oxide (rGO) in water, where BPEI solutions can produce stable dispersions that remain visually unchanged after four weeks of aging.[1] Its cationic nature underpins a key functional mechanism as an antimicrobial potentiator, where BPEI interacts synergistically with certain antibiotics against resistant bacterial strains.[3] For instance, against methicillin-resistant *Staphylococcus epidermidis* (MRSE) laboratory strains, BPEI with a molecular weight of 600 Da demonstrated a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 8 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 for strain MRSE 35984 and 2 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 for a methicillin-susceptible strain (MSSE 12228).[3] When combined with \u03b2-lactam antibiotics, BPEI exhibits strong synergistic effects, significantly reducing the MIC of the antibiotics.[3] In checkerboard assays, the combination of 4 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 of 600-Da BPEI with oxacillin reduced the oxacillin MIC for MRSE 35984 from 32 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 to 4 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 (an 8-fold reduction) and for MRSE 29887 from 8 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 to 0.5 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 (a 16-fold reduction), with fractional inhibitory concentration index (FICI) values of 0.5 and 0.375, respectively, indicating synergy.[3] Similar synergy was observed with amoxicillin (FICI = 0.5 against MRSE 35984) and ampicillin (FICI = 0.31 against MRSE 29887), with the amoxicillin MIC lowered by up to 256-fold (from 64 to 0.25 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9) in combination with BPEI.[3] This synergistic mechanism appears specific, as no synergy was observed between BPEI and vancomycin (FICI = 1) or between BPEI and \u03b2-lactams against the susceptible MSSE 12228 strain that lacks the PBP2a resistance determinant (FICI = 1).[3] The antimicrobial action is associated with physical disruption of bacterial cells; treatment of MRSE 35984 with BPEI alone or in combination with oxacillin resulted in a statistically significant increase in average cell diameter to 1.00 \u00b1 0.10 \u03bcm and 1.08 \u00b1 0.15 \u03bcm, respectively, compared to 0.82 \u00b1 0.07 \u03bcm for untreated controls, suggesting cell wall or membrane damage.[3]\n\n### Efficacy\n\nBranched polyethylenimine (BPEI) effectively enables the dispersion of reduced graphite oxide (rGO) in water, with its performance influenced by molecular weight.[1][2] The initial dispersed concentration of rGO followed the trend CMC90/rGO \u2248 CMC250/rGO > BPEI270/rGO > BPEI25/rGO > PSS70/rGO > PSS250/rGO > PAA100/rGO > PAA250/rGO, indicating BPEI formulations achieved moderate initial loading.[1][2] The surface tension of 0.1 wt% BPEI solutions is reported to be approximately 71-72 mN m\u207b\u00b9, similar to water and other polyelectrolytes like PSS and CMC at that concentration.[1][2] Concurrently, BPEI demonstrates efficacy in disabling biological targets by synergistically restoring antibiotic susceptibility in resistant bacteria.[3] Against methicillin-resistant *Staphylococcus epidermidis* (MRSE) laboratory strain 35984, the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 600-Da BPEI was 8 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9, while for MRSE strain 29887 it was also 8 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9.[3] Checkerboard assays showed synergy between BPEI and oxacillin against both MRSE 35984 (Fractional Inhibitory Concentration Index, FICI = 0.5) and MRSE 29887 (FICI = 0.375).[3] In the presence of 4 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 BPEI, the oxacillin MIC for MRSE 35984 was reduced from 32 to 4 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 (an 8-fold reduction) and for MRSE 29887 from 8 to 0.5 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 (a 16-fold reduction).[3] Synergy was also observed with amoxicillin (FICI = 0.5) against MRSE 35984 and with ampicillin (FICI = 0.31) against MRSE 29887.[3] The MIC of amoxicillin was lowered by 256-fold (from 64 to 0.25 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9) when combined with 4 \u03bcg mL\u207b\u00b9 BPEI.[3] This disabling effect is specific to resistant strains, as BPEI did not potentiate \u03b2-lactam activity against a methicillin-susceptible *S. epidermidis* (MSSE) strain lacking PBP2a (FICI = 1).[3] Furthermore, BPEI's cationic properties are leveraged in drug delivery, as one gene delivery system encapsulated bPEI-condensed plasmid DNA into targeted stealth liposomes to combine bPEI's condensing and transfection properties with stealth characteristics.[4][5]\n\n### Safety\n\nBranched polyethylenimine (BPEI) exhibits a concentration-dependent cytotoxic profile, with its safety window defined by the relationship between its effective antimicrobial concentration and the threshold for mammalian cell toxicity.[3] In laboratory studies against methicillin-resistant *Staphylococcus epidermidis* (MRSE), the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 600-Da BPEI was 8 \u03bcg/mL for strains MRSE 35984 and MRSE 29887, while for a methicillin-susceptible strain (MSSE 12228) it was 2 \u03bcg/mL.[3] At these antibacterial concentrations, BPEI can induce significant morphological changes in bacterial cells; for MRSE 35984, treatment with BPEI alone significantly increased average cell diameter to 1.00 \u00b1 0.10 \u03bcm from a control size of 0.82 \u00b1 0.07 \u03bcm (ANOVA, p-value < 0.001).[3] This effect is mechanistically selective, as the same treatment did not cause a statistically significant change in cell size for the MSSE 12228 strain (0.81 \u00b1 0.09 \u03bcm with BPEI vs. 0.79 \u00b1 0.06 \u03bcm control; ANOVA, p-value > 0.001).[3] Checkerboard assays demonstrated strong synergy (Fractional Inhibitory Concentration Index, FICI < 0.5) between BPEI and oxacillin against MRSE 35984 (FICI = 0.5) and MRSE 29887 (FICI = 0.375), reducing the oxacillin MIC for MRSE 29887 from 8 \u03bcg/mL to 0.5 \u03bcg/mL in the presence of 4 \u03bcg/mL BPEI.[3] Synergy was also observed with amoxicillin (FICI = 0.5 against MRSE 35984) and ampicillin (FICI = 0.31 against MRSE 29887), and an additive effect was noted with linezolid (FICI = 0.75 against MRSE 29887).[3] This synergy is specific to resistant strains, as BPEI did not potentiate \u03b2-lactam activity against the susceptible MSSE 12228 strain, which lacks PBP2a (FICI = 1).[3] No synergistic interaction was observed between BPEI and vancomycin against the tested MRSE strains (FICI = 1).[3] When used in combination therapy, the effective antibiotic concentration can be drastically lowered; for instance, amoxicillin's MIC against MRSE 35984 was reduced 256-fold from 64 \u03bcg/mL to 0.25 \u03bcg/mL when combined with 4 \u03bcg/mL BPEI.[3] The primary safety concern for in vivo applications is BPEI's inherent cytotoxicity at higher concentrations, a well-documented challenge that has driven its formulation into delivery systems like stealth liposomes to mitigate toxicity while leveraging its transfection and condensing properties.[4][5]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nBlepharophimosis, ptosis, and epicanthus inversus syndrome (BPES) is a genetic disorder primarily affecting eyelid development, characterized by the triad of blepharophimosis (narrowing of the eye opening), ptosis (droopy eyelids), and an upward fold of skin from the lower eyelid near the inner corner known as epicanthus inversus.[6][7] The syndrome results from mutations in the FOXL2 gene, which is located on the long arm of chromosome 3 at position 3q22.3.[8][9] This gene provides instructions for synthesizing a protein active in the eyelids and ovaries, where it is involved in development and regulation.[8] Two clinical types are recognized: Type 1 BPES is associated with primary ovarian insufficiency leading to infertility, while Type 2 lacks this systemic association.[10][11][12] The eyelid abnormalities, which also include an increased distance between the inner corners of the eyes (telecanthus), limit the ability to open the eyes fully and can restrict vision.[6][7] Affected individuals are at increased risk for childhood-onset vision problems including nearsightedness (myopia), farsightedness (hyperopia), strabismus, and amblyopia (\"lazy eye\").[6][7] Other associated facial features can include a broad nasal bridge, low-set ears, and ectropion.[6][11] Management often involves surgical correction of the eyelid anomalies, with techniques described for medial epicanthoplasty and ptosis repair.[11] In the context of ophthalmic care, institutions like the Bascom Palmer Eye Institute (BPEI), part of the University of Miami Health System, represent centers providing advanced ophthalmic care.[13] This institute also hosts educational events for allied health professionals, such as the \"2024 Ophthalmic Allied Health Professionals Seminar\".[14]\n\n### Dose Response\n\nThe functional performance of branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI) as a dispersant for reduced graphite oxide (rGO) in water is influenced by its molecular weight, with higher molecular weight BPEI producing more concentrated and stable dispersions.[1][2] In a comparative study using a consistent 0.1 wt% polyelectrolyte concentration, BPEI with a molecular weight of ~270,000 Da (BPEI270) achieved an initial dispersed rGO concentration of approximately 0.022 mg/mL.[1] In contrast, a lower molecular weight BPEI variant (~25,000 Da, BPEI25) yielded a significantly lower initial concentration of about 0.014 mg/mL.[1] This performance differential was maintained over time, as the stability of the dispersions after four weeks of aging followed the same trend.[1] The charge density of BPEI, which is pH-dependent, also plays a critical role in its interaction with rGO.[1][2] At a concentration of 0.1 wt%, BPEI solutions exhibit a surface tension similar to that of water, approximately 71-72 mN/m, which is distinct from the behavior of other polyelectrolytes like poly(acrylic acid) (PAA).[1][2] The study investigated BPEI's performance across different pH levels to modulate its charge density.[1] The results indicate that the dispersion efficacy and stability are functions of the pH-dependent ionization state of the BPEI, affecting its electrostatic and potentially hydrophobic interactions with the rGO sheets.[1][2] Therefore, optimizing both the molecular weight and the solution pH (governing charge density) of BPEI is essential for maximizing the concentration and long-term stability of rGO aqueous dispersions.[1][2]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The corpus exhibits significant gaps in tier distribution, with only 20% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a majority (60%) of sources having an unknown tier, limiting the ability to assess evidence strength. The pipeline detected no direct contradictions in the evidence. The large proportion of unknown-tier sources and the absence of coverage on the topic of critical analysis or limitations represent a notable completeness gap in the synthesized evidence base.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 b5e9c79d47fea176...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (custom: scope_query_validator: 1 kept / 0 dropped).\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2-exp (multi-section: outline + 5 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: qwen/qwen3-8b (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per custom template. gregor.primc@ijs.si\nFind articles by [Gregor Primc](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Primc%20G\"\\[Author\\])\n1, [Miran Mozetič](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Mozeti%C4%8D%20M\"\\[Author\\])\n### Miran Mozetič\n1Department of Surface Engineering, Jozef Stefan Institute, Jamova cesta 39, 1000 Ljubljana, Slovenia; gregor.primc@ijs.si\nFind articles by [Miran Mozetič](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Mozeti%C4%8D%20M\"\\[Author\\])\n1,*\nEditor: Norbert Robert Radek1\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n1Department of Surface Engineering, Jozef Stefan Institute, Jamova cesta 39, 1000 Ljubljana, Slovenia; gregor.primc@ijs.si\n*\nCorrespondence: miran.mozetic@guest.arnes.si\n#### Roles\n**Norbert Robert Radek** : Academic Editor\nReceived 2024 Feb 22; Revised 2024 Mar 19; Accepted 2024 Mar 24; Collection date 2024 Apr.\n© 2024 by the authors.\nLicensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland. This article is an open access article distributed under the terms and conditions of the Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) license (<https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/>).\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC11012850 PMID: [38612009](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38612009/)\n##  Abstract\nIn this study, recent advances in tailoring the surface properties of polymers for the optimization of the adhesion of various coatings by non-equilibrium gaseous plasma are reviewed, and important findings are stressed. Different authors have used various experimental setups and reported results that scatter significantly and are sometimes contradictory. The correlations between the processing parameters and the adhesion are drawn, and discrepancies are explained. Many authors have explained improved adhesion with the adjustment of the surface free energy or wettability of the polymer substrate and the surface tension of liquids used for the deposition of thin films. The adhesion force between the polymer substrate and the coating does not always follow the evolution of the surface wettability, which is explained by several effects, including the aging effects due to the hydrophobic recovery and the formation of an interlayer rich in loosely bonded low molecular weight fragments.\n**Keywords:** polymers, gaseous plasma, wettability, adhesion, coatings\n##  1. Introduction\nThe adhesion of various coatings on polymer substrates has attracted significant attention from the scientific community due to the need to invent reliable, inexpensive, and ecologically benign methods for optimizing the adhesion force and durability of products made from adhered materials. The interaction between the substrate and the coating has also been studied, and numerous mechanisms have been proposed [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11012850/#B1-materials-17-01494)]. Briefly, the mechanisms could be categorized as mechanical coupling and chemical interaction. The mechanical coupling will be enhanced for substrates of appropriate roughness, while the chemical interaction will be optimized if the deposited material forms covalent or other strong bonds with the functional groups on the polymer surface. A crucial property that affects both mechanical and chemical interaction is the ability of a coating to fill any gaps or pores on the surface of materials to be joined. The effect is illustrated in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11012850/#materials-17-01494-f001).\n### Figure 1.\n[![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/0d05/11012850/c0e5c980121f/materials-17-01494-g001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11012850_materials-17-01494-g001.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11012850/figure/materials-17-01494-f001/)\nInfluence of morphology on the optimal (**a**) and inadequate (**b**) adhesion between two materials.\n[Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11012850/#materials-17-01494-f001)a illustrates the case of optimal adhesion. The materials stick well because all gaps or other morphological features are filled. The optimal adhesion is rarely achieved since the coating does not wet the substrate thoroughly, i.e., does not fill tiny pores or gaps. The inadequate adhesion is ill"

outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_7\findings.md:10:  BUG-M-202 is confirmed. `extract_numeric_claims()` only attempts extraction after `_normalize_predicate()` matches one of the hard-coded obesity/cardiometabolic predicate strings. Because the clinical AF anticoagulation corpus discusses endpoints such as stroke, systemic embolism, major bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage, INR/TTR, CHA2DS2-VASc, and HAS-BLED rather than "weight loss" or "hba1c reduction", the detector returns `numeric_claims=0 contradictions=0` before any numeric values are considered. The fix should not replace deterministic numeric comparison with unconstrained LLM judgment. It should add generic numeric-claim mining, then use domain YAML predicate hints for normalization, unit policy, and endpoint aliases.
outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_7\findings.md:19:The detector is documented as a general numeric contradiction detector, but its extraction gate is domain-specific. In `src/polaris_graph/retrieval/contradiction_detector.py`, `_EFFICACY_PREDICATES` and `_SAFETY_PREDICATES` contain a narrow clinical-metabolic list: weight loss/body weight, HbA1c/A1c, blood pressure, LDL/cholesterol, MACE/mortality, nausea/vomiting, discontinuation, adverse events, pancreatitis, hypoglycemia, and thyroid C-cell (`:78-93`). `_normalize_predicate()` only returns a predicate if one of those literal substrings appears in the quote (`:134-140`).
outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_7\findings.md:21:That means a quote can contain clear numeric contradictions and still be invisible if it uses another domain's endpoint vocabulary. The current AF anticoagulation run demonstrates the failure: `outputs/honest_sweep_r6_validation/clinical/clinical_afib_anticoagulation/run_log.txt` logs `[contradict] numeric_claims=0 contradictions=0` even though the query domain naturally contains stroke, systemic embolism, bleeding, INR/TTR, risk-score, and hazard/relative-risk endpoints.

outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_5\findings.md:22:`live_qwen_judge.py` asks Qwen for five per-axis verdicts: `citation_tightness`, `hedging_appropriateness`, `tone_consistency`, `flow`, and `completeness`. Qwen cannot inspect the evidence pool, provenance token spans, contradiction records, or tier-distribution telemetry.
